title: Food, Putrefaction, Gold, Milk, and Blood
author: Marsilio Ficino

body.text: Book II of the Liber de vita treats aging as an ecology of intake, decay, and preservation. The deeper argument is about preservation against putrefaction, incorruptibility as amaterial-spiritual ideal, and vital transfer as the mechanism by which life passes through matter. De vita II.6 opens the material ecology: life is acontinuous replacement problem. The body is always spending its substance and must be replenished through food. Eating is not lifestyle. It is the daily rebuilding of life itself. Corpus semper substantiam suam expendit et repleri debet. Cibus non est vitae genus sed ipsa vita reficienda. The body is always spending its substance and must be replenished. Food is not alifestyle choice but the very rebuilding of life. De vita II.7 names the enemy: putrefaction. Putrefaction is the breakdown of ordered life against corruption. Bad foods and bad regions accelerate this breakdown. Putrefaction matters because old age is not abstract decline. It is the breakdown of the body'scapacity to preserve spirit. The enemy is not time but decay. Putredo est dissolutio vitae ordinatae. Senectus est defectus spiritus conservandi. Hostis non tempus est sed putredo. Putrefaction is the dissolution of ordered life. Old age is the failure to preserve spirit. The enemy is not time but putrefaction. The concept of putrefaction links Book II to the alchemical tradition. In alchemy, putrefaction is the first stage of transformation — the breakdown of the old form so that anew form can emerge. Ficino is not using the word casually. He is drawing on atradition in which decay and renewal are two phases of the same process. Putredo in alchemia est prima transformatio. Ficinus hanc traditionem sequitur. The foods and regions that accelerate putrefaction are those that are already close to decay themselves. Old meat, stale bread, stagnant water — these carry the seeds of corruption into the body. The foods that resist putrefaction are those that are fresh, warm, and alive. Ficino'sdietary advice is not arbitrary. It follows from his understanding of life as astruggle against dissolution. Cibi qui putredinem accelerant sunt qui iam prope corruptionem sunt. Cibi qui putredini resistunt sunt recentes, calidi, vivi. The body that eats decaying food is eating death. The body that eats living food is eating life. This is not ametaphor. It is the physiological foundation of Ficino'slongevity practices. Putredo in alchemia est prima transformatio. Ficinus hanc traditionem sequitur. De vita II.8 and II.9 give specific guidance for the elderly diet: wine, wheat, spices, and cordials that preserve warmth and moisture. The logic is consistent with Book I: the instrument must be maintained through what enters it. The elderly diet is designed to counteract the natural drying and cooling of the body. Wine warms. Wheat nourishes. Spices stimulate. Cordials restore. Each element of the diet addresses aspecific aspect of the aging process. De vita II.10 introduces asubstance that stands outside the ordinary economy of decay:
"Gold is incorruptible, solar, bright, noble, stable, anti-putrefactive."

Aurum est incorruptibile, solare, splendidum, nobile, stabile, antiputredinosum. Gold images permanence. It does not rust, rot, or decay. In Ficino'ssymbolic-medical world, gold belongs both to the preservation of the body and to the solarisation of the spirit that Book III will describe. The same gold that preserves the aging body will later become avehicle for celestial reception. Aurum est imago incorruptibilitatis. Idem aurum quod corpus in II conservat in III vehiculum receptionis coelestis fit. Gold is not merely asymbol. Ficino believed that gold could be ingested in aform that would transfer its incorruptibility to the body. The spirit of gold, separated from its gross matter through sublimation, could be taken as medicine. This is not magic. It is applied alchemy — the belief that the qualities of asubstance can be transferred to the one who ingests it. Spiritus auri per sublimationem separatus ut medicina sumi potest. Non est magia sed alchemia applicata. The gold that preserves the body in Book II is the same gold that will become avehicle for celestial reception in Book III. In De vita III.14, gold appears again as asubstance belonging to the Sun. The solarisation of the spirit that Book III describes — the process of making the spirit solar through the reception of celestial influence — is prefigured in Book II by the ingestion of gold, which makes the body more like gold: incorruptible, stable, solar. Aurum in II corpus conservat. Aurum in III spiritum solarem facit. Idem aurum, idem principium. The same gold, the same principle. The continuity between Book II and Book III is visible in the simplest substance. De vita II.11 extends the logic of preservation into vital transfer. Human milk and blood can carry life, youth, and substance from one body to another. This is not apractical recommendation. It is evidence of how far Ficino'stheory of vital transfer goes. Ficino'sdiscussion of human milk and blood is often the most startling passage in Book II for modern readers. The idea that drinking the blood of ayoung person could extend life seems barbaric. But Ficino is not recommending vampirism. He is working within atheoretical framework in which the qualities of asubstance — including its vitality — can be transferred through ingestion. If the spirit is avapour of blood, then the blood of ayoung person, whose spirit is still strong, carries more life than the blood of an old person, whose spirit is depleted. Sanguis iuvenis plus vitae habet quam sanguis senis. Haec non est magia sed theoria spiritalis. The theory is consistent. It is not sensational. The same principle that makes gold amedicine because of its incorruptibility makes young blood amedicine because of its vitality. Both are applications of the same logic: like preserves like. Lac humanum et sanguis vitam, iuventutem, substantiam de corpore ad corpus transferre possunt. Life is not an abstract property. It is asubstance that can be carried by blood, by milk, by breath. The vitality of ayoung person can, in principle, be transferred to an older person through the medium of the body'sfluids. This is the logical extension of the spiritus doctrine: if the spirit is avapour of blood, then the quality of the blood determines the quality of the spirit, and the quality of the spirit determines the quality of life. Si spiritus est vapor sanguinis, qualitas sanguinis qualitatem spiritus determinat. De vita II.12 adds that dwelling and custom matter as much as ingestion. Longevity is not what you take in but where you are, what you do, who you are with. Place, climate, habit, and continuity structure the conditions under which life is preserved or lost. Habitatio et consuetudo non minus quam cibus valent. Longaevitas non est ingestio sed locus, climate, habitus, continentia. The later chapters of Book II begin to lean toward Book III. De vita II.13 states that planets support bodily members; the body ages within acosmic ecology. De vita II.14 describes how Venus brings green fields and restorative delight — pleasure, greenness, and beauty become medicine when rightly ordered. De vita II.15 gives Mercury'scontribution: odours, song, and medicines that operate on the subtle body. Planetae membra corporis sustinent. Venus campos virentes affert. Mercurius odores, cantus, medicinas subtiles. De vita II.16 returns to atheme from Book I: thought and sex both spend vital force. In old age, expenditure matters more than ever. De vita II.18 identifies odours and honey as substances that preserve and nourish the spirit directly — abridge from ordinary to subtle medicine. De vita II.19 introduces the Medicine of the Magi, which anticipates Book III'smagical turn. De vita II.20 — "Each seventh year brings amajor bodily change because rulership returns from Saturn to the Moon. These are climacteric years."
"Natural lifespan can be moved nearer or farther by stars and material things. Doctors and astrologers both matter."

The emphasis on dwelling and custom in II.12 connects this essay to the broader argument of the Liber de vita. The scholar who lives in the wrong place will age faster, not because the place is unhealthy in itself but because it does not resonate with his nature. The preservation of life requires alignment — alignment of food with body, of place with person, of habit with nature. Conservatio vitae congruentiam requirit. Congruentia cibi cum corpore, loci cum persona, habitus cum natura. The theme that unites these chapters is preservation against corruption. Gold symbolises incorruptibility. Milk and blood represent life as transferable nourishment. Putrefaction is the breaking point — the moment when the body can no longer preserve the spirit. Ficino'smaterial ecology is atheory of how life resists its own dissolution through right intake, right place, and right habit. The body that eats well, dwells well, and lives well is abody that holds its form against time. Theoria Ficini est quomodo vita dissolutionem resistit per rectum cibum, rectum locum, rectum habitum. Corpus quod bene edit, bene habitat, bene vivit, formam suam contra tempus tenet. The body that eats well, dwells well, and lives well is abody that holds its form against time. This is the message of Book II'sstrangest chapters — amessage that is not about diet or gold or milk but about the fundamental nature of life as an ordered resistance to disorder. Life is order against chaos. Preservation is resistance against dissolution.
